title: Being Heard
body:
Musicians and instrument makers are closely connected. Musicians need instrument makers to provide their tools for expression, and makers need musicians to evaluate, critique, and give voice to their work. As the world of music making changes, musicians and makers work together to adapt.

Guitarists and luthiers found themselves in just such a situation when, in the late 1800s, the guitar competed with the louder banjo and mandolin for the public’s attention. And, as musical ensembles and venues grew in size, the guitar proved too quiet to accompany louder instruments. Luthiers introduced new, volume-enhancing models, including the guitars shown here, to help musicians be heard in, and even above, a crowd.

content.title: The Need to Be Heard
content.body:
In 1854, C.F. Martin’s product line featured guitars in sizes 5 (with a body 11 ¼” wide) through 0 (13 ½” wide). Called parlor guitars, these typically used gut strings and were suitable for small, intimate performance situations. As louder instruments and larger venues became the norm in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, luthiers built larger, and louder, guitars. By 1902 Martin had added the 00 and 000 sizes to their lineup, and in 1931 released the Dreadnought, or D, model at 15 5/8” wide. With enough volume to play alongside other fretted instruments, the Dreadnought quickly became a fixture of American guitar playing.

Meanwhile, the Gibson Mandolin-Guitar Company introduced newly designed instruments that borrowed the concept of a carved top and back from violin making. These archtop instruments were rigid enough to need only minimal internal bracing, making them louder than comparably sized flattop guitars. Even so, Gibson released ever-larger instruments, up to 18” wide by 1934. With their huge sound, these archtops were widely adopted by jazz guitarists and other musicians playing with large ensembles.

images.caption: Wes Montgomery. Courtesy of Indiana Historical Society, M1178.
Guitar size and body shape comparison.
Martin Guitar Factory, Nazareth, Pennsylvania, 1920s. Courtesy of Ron Middlebrook.

content.title: New Materials
content.body:
Through the 20th and 21st centuries, radical advances in materials have allowed luthiers to supplement, or even replace, the wood in their guitars to boost volume. Among the first successful designs incorporating alternative materials was the National String Instrument Corporation resonator guitar, made in 1927. Production models were fashioned from cast aluminum and featured internal cones to resonate with and amplify their sound. In 1966, helicopter engineer Charles Kaman and the Ovation Guitar Company released their first guitars. These featured a parabolic bowl-shaped back made from Lyrachord, a form of fiberglass, giving them noticeably more volume than comparably sized all-wood guitars.

In the early 1980s, plasma physicist and RainSong founder Dr. John Decker explored alternate materials after seeing a guitarist caught in a rainstorm in Hawaii. Decker engineered a guitar made of graphite fiber, acoustically similar to spruce. Released in 1995, these guitars were made without any internal bracing which, combined with graphite’s lightness and rigidity, reduced damping and made them louder. And, true to his inspiration, Decker’s guitar was impervious to environmental changes – including sudden rainstorms.

images.caption: Charles Kaman with an Ovation Guitar.

content.title: Building for the Player
content.body:
Through a combination of innovation and close interaction with musicians, luthiers have developed a huge range of techniques for building guitars that impact everything about the instrument.

One of the defining features of the modern American flat-top acoustic guitar is the X-brace, attributed to C.F. Martin in the early 1840s. With this bracing, the top of the guitar is primarily supported by two wooden struts overlapping in an “X” shape. The X-brace strengthens the guitar’s top, which proved crucial as steel strings became popular in the early 20th century. The additional tension of steel strings also led luthiers to change the instrument’s neck. The addition of a truss rod lets luthiers stabilize the neck and gives players the ability to adjust the height of the strings relative to the guitar’s fretboard.

Further innovations allow players to change the position of the neck itself relative to the strings or change the angle of the neck relative to the body, letting a single guitar adjust to many different playing styles.

images.caption: Luthier cutting a channel to place the truss rod in the guitar neck. Credit: Luciano Queiroz.
Martin Guitar X-Bracing. Courtesy of Martin Guitar Co.

content.title: Harp Guitar
content.body: Though their exact origins are uncertain, harp guitars – guitars with a number of additional “floating” unstopped strings – seem to have appeared in Europe in the early to mid-1800s as a means to extend the guitar’s low end and increase volume. Chris Knutsen, a designer based in Port Townsend, WA, began creating harp guitars in the late 1890s. This example features five strings mounted over a hollow arm extending from the guitar’s body.

content.title: Style 3 Hawaiian Guitar
content.body: Guitars were brought to the Hawaiian Islands in the 19th century by European colonialists. Rather than adopt the European tuning and upright posture, many Hawaiians preferred an open tuning and laid the instrument across their laps, using a slide to change chords. This style of playing became popular in mainland vaudeville circuits in the early 20th century, particularly after Hawaii’s annexation by the U.S. in 1898. Guitars with squared hollow necks – known as Hawaiian or steel guitars – were built on the mainland to support the growing number of musicians seeking to perform in this style. Kona guitars were built in Los Angeles, CA, likely by Herman Weissenborn, for guitarist and teacher Charles. S. De Lano

content.title: Spanish-Style Resonator Guitar Prototype
content.body: In the 1920s, vaudeville steel guitarist George Beauchamp asked inventor John Dopyera to devise a way to amplify the sound of a guitar. Dopyera experimented with placing resonating cones in the body of the instrument, and in 1927 he and Beauchamp were making resonator guitars with Hawaiian-style bodies under the name National String Instrument Company. This example, made of nickel silver, was National's first prototype guitar with three resonators. Its Hawaiian-style square neck was replaced with a Spanish-style one at the request of guitarist Waltimor Shablowski, and it subsequently became the prototype for National’s Spanish-style resonator guitars.
content.credit: On Loan from the Collection of David Flood

content.title: Balladeer Guitar
content.body: In 1966, helicopter engineer Charles Kaman and the Ovation Guitar Company released their first guitars. These featured a parabolic bowl-shaped back made from Lyrachord, a form of fiberglass based on materials used in helicopter rotors and other aerospace components, giving them noticeably more volume than comparably sized all-wood guitars.
content.credit: On Loan from Bill Kaman, Ovation Instruments

content.title: DR 1100 Guitar
content.body: In the early 1980s, plasma physicist and RainSong founder Dr. John Decker explored alternate materials after seeing a guitarist caught in a rainstorm in Hawaii. Decker engineered a guitar made of graphite fiber, acoustically similar to spruce. Released in 1995, these guitars were made without any internal bracing which, combined with graphite’s lightness and rigidity, reduced damping and made them louder. And, true to his inspiration, Decker’s guitar was impervious to environmental changes – including sudden rainstorms.

content.title: D-28 Dreadnought Guitar with Pickup
content.body: C.F. Martin initially developed the large "dreadnought" guitar body type in 1916 for the Oliver Ditson Company, but it sold very poorly. Martin revisited the design in 1931 and, with some modifications, released it under their own name as the D-1 and D-2 models. The dreadnought body, prized for its projection and rich tone, has become the standard instrument across many genres.
content.credit: On Loan from C.F. Martin & Co. Collections

content.title: OM-28VR Orchestra Model Guitar
content.body: The OM, or Orchestra Model, was designed in 1929 by Martin for star banjoist Perry Bechtel, who was looking to switch to the guitar. To make the guitar feel more like a banjo, which has a longer neck and smaller body, its scale was lengthened from 24.9” to 25.4” and the body was shortened to meet the neck at the 14th fret. Guitars with the OM name were produced between 1930 and 1933, at which point many of its features were incorporated into 000 models. OMs were produced again in limited numbers starting in 1969 before fully reentering the Martin catalog in the 1990s.
content.credit: On Loan from C.F. Martin & Co. Collections

content.title: 00-42 Parlor Guitar
content.body: IThe 00 body type, known as the Grand Concert, was the largest instrument available from C.F. Martin through the early 20th century but is now among the smallest. The second part of the model name, in this case 42, refers to the instrument's style, or level of appointment. Style 42 was, at the time, the top of Martin's line and featured pearl inlay, ivory components, and an ebony fingerboard. The two additional holes on the headstock of this instrument indicate that it had previously been adapted for use with eight strings.
content.credit: On Loan from C.F. Martin & Co. Collections

content.title: 1887 Style New Model Parlor Guitar
content.body: Washburn guitars, along with mandolins, banjos, and zithers, were manufactured by Chicago-based Lyon & Healy beginning in the late 1880s. This parlor guitar measures 14” in width and features sections of pearl inlay on the fingerboard.
content.credit: On Loan from the Personal Collection of Rudy Schlacter, Washburn Intl.

content.title: L-1 Guitar
content.body: Gibson’s L-1 was introduced in 1902 and measured 13 ½" at its widest point. Despite its size, the archtop design gave the instrument more volume than a comparable flattop. Gibson’s 1903 catalog is careful to point out that the guitar should never be held flat against the player’s body, as that would impede the instrument’s vibration.

content.title: L-5 Guitar
content.body: In 1919, Gibson hired luthier and sound engineer Lloyd Loar to help design new instruments and revive flagging sales. Loar’s L-5 replaced the traditional round sound hole with violin-like f-holes and floated the fingerboard above the guitar’s top, among other changes. Initially released at 16” wide, the L5 was expanded to 17” in 1934.
content.credit: On Loan from the Guitar Center Vintage Collection

content.label: Chris Martin IV
content.summary: Chris Martin IV, chairman of C.F. Martin & Company, discusses the company's approach to building acoustic guitars in the 1920s and 30s.

content.label: Bob Taylor
content.summary: Bob Taylor, founder of Taylor Guitars, shares how he built his company and what goals he keeps in mind when making guitars.

content.label: Bob Benedetto
content.summary: Bob Benedetto, founder of Benedetto Guitars, shares his reasons for using the "f-hole" in archtop guitars.

content.label: Pete Seeger
content.summary: Legendary folk musician Pete Seeger reminisces about his 12-string guitar.
